title: Picking Up the Pieces
bullets: Mao Zedong died in 1976 leaving a country poorer per person than most of sub-Saharan Africa, universities closed for a decade, the economy running on ration coupons.
Hua Guofeng, Mao's chosen heir, arrested the Gang of Four but clung to the Two Whatevers and had no answer to a country that needed change, lasting about two years.
Deng Xiaoping, purged twice and rehabilitated for the third time in 1977 at seventy-two, became paramount leader without ever holding the top title.
The slogan 'practice is the sole criterion of truth' was a precision political weapon: if results, not Mao's words, decided truth, the Two Whatevers had no ground to stand on.
Deng's earthier version — 'seek truth from facts', the cat that catches mice can be capitalist — was the whole revolution in one sentence.
The December 1978 Third Plenum shifted the party's central task from class struggle to economic construction, the conventional start of the reform era.
Boluan Fanzheng reversed Cultural Revolution verdicts and the 1981 Resolution on Party History rendered the verdict the regime still holds: Mao was 70 percent right, 30 percent wrong.
The electrician Wei Jingsheng demanded democracy as a Fifth Modernization on Democracy Wall and got fifteen years; the 1979 deal was set — prosperity for an absolute monopoly on power.

title: The Household Responsibility System
bullets: The first and largest poverty reduction was rural and began as a crime: eighteen starving households in Xiaogang, Anhui secretly divided collective land in 1978, fingerprinting a contract to raise each other's children if executed.
The next harvest beat the previous five combined — proof nothing was wrong with Chinese agriculture except the incentive, the answer the famine chapter of the last book left open.
The Household Responsibility System was invented illegally by farmers, covered by brave provincial officials, and only afterward ratified by Beijing, which took the credit.
Within six years collective farming was gone and rural incomes had roughly doubled, with no foreign investment and no Special Economic Zones in it at all.
Surplus farm labor, blocked from the cities by the hukou registration system, went into township and village enterprises that outgrew state industry.
Hu Yaobang, the genuine liberal party chief, drove the rehabilitations and intellectual openness and was purged in 1987 for it.
Zhao Ziyang, the brilliant technocrat premier, designed and ran economic policy until his fall over Tiananmen in 1989.
The arc from the Xiaogang fingerprints to Zhao Ziyang under house arrest is the book's arc: an economic miracle and a political dead end from the same root.

title: Special Economic Zones and the Open Door
bullets: Reform was run as walled experiments because the leadership did not trust it and could not admit, ideologically, what it was doing.
Four Special Economic Zones opened on the southern coast in 1979–80; Shenzhen was chosen not for resources but for adjacency to Hong Kong's capital and factory know-how.
For the first decade most foreign investment was not Western multinationals but overseas Chinese capital moving factories a few miles across a border into a workforce that cost a tenth as much.
Shekou ran up the slogan 'time is money, efficiency is life' — a death sentence a decade earlier, now state policy.
'Shenzhen speed' — a building floor every few days — turned a few hundred thousand people into a city of over ten million within a generation.
China imported an industrial economy that already existed next door rather than inventing one, doing the visible part faster than Meiji Japan or South Korea.
The question 'is Shenzhen socialist or capitalist?' was the live fault line of elite politics for fifteen years; the evasive answer was 'socialism with Chinese characteristics'.
India had democracy first and a slow half-reformed economy; China had economic reform first and no democracy, and for thirty years grew far faster — the book's recurring contrast.

title: The Urban Reforms and the First Strains
bullets: Rural reform was easy; urban and industrial reform was the hard problem, and its half-failure produced the inflation, corruption, and fracture that exploded in 1989.
The dual-track price system gave every commodity two prices at once — a low planned price and a high market price — to keep the plan stable while a market grew at the margin.
The gap created the era's defining corruption: officials and their children bought at the planned price and sold at the market price, the practice called guandao.
The word 'princelings' — leaders' children converting power into fortune — enters Chinese political life here and never leaves it.
A 1988 push to free prices triggered double-digit inflation and panic bank runs, forcing a retreat that discredited the reformers and split the leadership openly.
The 1980s 'culture fever' flooded China with translated Western thought and the question of whether Chinese tradition itself blocked modernization.
The 1988 TV series River Elegy, watched by hundreds of millions, was a direct descendant of the New Culture Movement's argument seventy years earlier.
By spring 1989: a fast, unequal, inflationary boom that had enriched the connected and frightened everyone else, run by a leadership at war with itself.

title: Tiananmen 1989
bullets: This is the hinge: the 1979 bargain — prosperity for a monopoly on power — was challenged head-on and defended with the army.
Mourning for the purged liberal Hu Yaobang, who died of a heart attack in April 1989, became the largest protest movement in PRC history.
The demands were mostly against corruption, inflation, guandao, and the princelings, not for abstract Western democracy — and the movement drew in workers, which made it lethal to the leadership.
A hunger strike timed to Gorbachev's first Sino-Soviet summit humiliated the government before the world's press already in Beijing.
Zhao Ziyang argued for conciliation, went to the square and told students 'We have come too late', was purged, and spent his last sixteen years under house arrest.
Martial law was declared; on the night of June 3–4 the People's Liberation Army cleared central Beijing by force, mostly on the approach avenues, not the square itself.
The death toll is genuinely unknown — the government says 200–300, other estimates run from several hundred to well over a thousand; the state has erased the event for thirty-five years.
The party concluded political liberalization is an existential threat and growth is what buys consent — the bet that defines every later chapter.

title: The Southern Tour and the Capitalist Turn
bullets: China froze for two and a half years after 1989 as the entire Communist world outside it — the Berlin Wall, Eastern Europe, then the USSR itself — collapsed.
Deng's lesson from the Soviet collapse, the book's key strategic judgment: the USSR died reforming politics without delivering goods; only undeniable growth could save the party.
In 1992 Deng, eighty-seven and holding no office, made the Southern Tour to the boom zones, going over the central leadership's head to relaunch reform.
Later in 1992 the Party Congress adopted the 'socialist market economy', ending the thirty-year question of whether markets were ideologically allowed.
'Xiahai' — jumping into the sea of private business — captured the decade's cultural turn toward money-making.
Jiang Zemin and the technocrat premier Zhu Rongji executed the takeoff; the overlooked 1994 tax-sharing reform recentralized revenue and built the modern state's fiscal machine.
Deng Xiaoping died in February 1997 and nothing happened — the proof his deepest achievement was a system that could transfer power without killing anyone at the top.
After two thousand years in which the strongman's death meant crisis, a paramount leader died and the machine simply kept running.

title: Joining the World
bullets: Hong Kong reverted in 1997 after 156 years, ending the colonial rule begun with the First Opium War, under a 'one country, two systems' promise to 2047.
The bet was that Hong Kong's freedoms could be quarantined behind a system boundary the way the Special Economic Zones had quarantined capitalism; it did not hold.
By not devaluing the yuan during the 1997–98 Asian Financial Crisis, China won regional credibility and a lasting suspicion of open capital markets.
Zhu Rongji's 'grasp the big, let go of the small' restructuring laid off tens of millions of state workers, ending the Maoist iron rice bowl — executed by the party itself.
The state privatized urban housing in 1998, creating overnight the property market that became households' main wealth and the economy's central vulnerability.
Accession to the World Trade Organization in 2001 locked in market access and quadrupled exports in under a decade, hollowing manufacturing across the developed world.
WTO entry was championed on the theory trade would liberalize China politically — the era's central failed prediction; China integrated and grew more authoritarian.
The 1999 ban on Falun Gong, suppressed simply for proving it could organize millions outside the party, showed which lesson 1989 had actually taught.

title: The Factory of the World
bullets: Under Hu Jintao and premier Wen Jiabao China became, in fact not slogan, the factory of the world and accumulated the contradictions the next leader would ride to power.
The engine was 200–300 million migrant workers, legally non-residents where they worked because of the hukou system — cheap, mobile, disenfranchised, by design.
India was also growing fast in these years but as a democracy where the rural poor vote, which made a Chinese hukou impossible and Indian growth slower and less brutal.
Roughly 800 million people were lifted out of extreme poverty over the reform era — broadly real, the largest and fastest in history, though economists argue the decomposition.
Inequality rose to among the world's highest; Hu's answer, 'harmonious society', named the fracture without the remedies 1989 had forbidden.
The world's largest high-speed rail network and the physical infrastructure of a developed economy were built within a single working life.
The scandal genre — poisoned infant formula sickening 300,000 babies, collapsing buildings — traced to an economy moving faster than any accountability the system allowed.
The 2003 SARS cover-up was the clearest warning of where that combination led, and the direct rehearsal for 2020.

title: The Olympics and the Global Crisis
bullets: 2008 is the inflection point: the arrival staged for the world, the Western model that blew up, and the response that mortgaged the future.
The Beijing Summer Olympics opening in the Bird's Nest, watched by an estimated four billion, was the emotional payoff of thirty years of the bargain — the century of humiliation declared over.
Three months earlier a Sichuan earthquake killed nearly 70,000; disproportionately schoolchildren, crushed in 'tofu-dreg' schools while sturdier buildings stood.
Ai Weiwei, who compiled a list of the dead children's names, was beaten and detained — the model's costs acquiring a body count the public could count.
In September the Western financial system collapsed, Chinese exports fell off a cliff, tens of thousands of factories closed, twenty million-plus migrants lost jobs.
The four-trillion-yuan stimulus, one of history's largest relative to the economy, kept China growing through the global recession and pulled the world along.
China emerged in 2010 as the world's second-largest economy, passing Japan, whose post-1990 stagnation it told itself it would avoid.
The stimulus built the trap: growth dependent on ever-rising debt and construction, the ghost cities and developer time bombs of the later chapters.

title: The Disaffected Decade and the Bo Xilai Affair
bullets: The system's hangover: undeniable costs papered over with debt, a brief semi-public internet square, and a succession crisis that produced Xi Jinping.
Ghost cities and a local-government debt mountain were the post-stimulus landscape — construction as an end in itself because building kept the growth numbers up.
Weibo created the first fast national semi-public square in PRC history, where the 2011 Wenzhou high-speed-rail crash and its cover-up went viral before the censors caught up.
Anonymous calls for 'Jasmine' gatherings in 2011 produced almost no protesters but a panicky crackdown — the disproportion the tell of elite anxiety.
The jailed dissident Liu Xiaobo, lead author of Charter 08, won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2010 and died in custody in 2017, showing the 1989 line still held absolutely.
Bo Xilai built a Mao-nostalgic populist power base in Chongqing and was campaigning, in a system where campaigning is forbidden, for a seat at the apex.
The affair detonated when his police chief Wang Lijun fled to a US consulate with evidence Bo's wife had murdered a British businessman.
Bo's purge cleared the path, and the elite chose Xi Jinping as a safe dull compromise — the greatest misreading in modern Chinese politics.

title: Xi Jinping Takes Power
bullets: The elite expected another consensus technocrat; within five years Xi Jinping had concentrated more personal power than anyone since Mao.
The anti-corruption campaign's genius was that the corruption was completely real — over a million officials disciplined, including the security overlord Zhou Yongkang.
It was simultaneously a genuine attack on corruption and the most effective purge of rivals in modern Chinese politics, and the two functions were inseparable.
The 'Chinese Dream' of national rejuvenation fused party rule to the century-of-humiliation memory, the same emotional engine Mao had used in 1949.
Document No. 9 in 2013 forbade promoting constitutional democracy, civil society, and a free press, and the Weibo opening was systematically closed.
The 2015 '709' crackdown gutted the small community of human-rights lawyers who had tried to use the regime's own courts.
In 2018 the constitutional two-term limit on the presidency was abolished, undoing Deng's solution to the two-thousand-year problem of what happens when the strongman dies.
The one-child policy was finally ended in 2015–16, not from liberalization but demographic panic — the workforce was already shrinking.

title: The Belt and Road and the Tech Ambition
bullets: Power projected outward and downward at once — a bid to reshape the global economy and to build the most sophisticated apparatus of social control ever attempted.
The Belt and Road Initiative, launched 2013, was the largest development-finance program since the Marshall Plan and an outlet for the steel and cement the home economy could no longer absorb.
Careful research found the 'debt-trap diplomacy' thesis mostly overstated — the Hambantota port case is the totem — while the strategic-influence reality is genuine.
China built a near-cashless economy on mobile payment by QR code through Alibaba's Alipay and Tencent's WeChat, leapfrogging credit cards entirely.
The platform economy and Made in China 2025 coexisted with a methodical space program that landed the first probe ever on the far side of the Moon in 2019.
The same decade built the most comprehensive domestic surveillance system in history; the unified national 'social credit score' was largely a Western dystopian myth, the blacklists real.
In Xinjiang from around 2017 the state subjected the Uyghurs to mass internment, surveillance, forced labor, and birth suppression.
The numbers and the word are contested — around a million or more detained; the US calls it genocide, others crimes against humanity, China denies it entirely.

title: Hong Kong and Taiwan
bullets: The two places where 'one country, two systems' met its test and the regime's core contradiction became impossible to paper over.
The 2014 Umbrella Movement, a 79-day occupation demanding genuine suffrage, won nothing but radicalized a generation.
The 2015 abduction of five Hong Kong booksellers into mainland custody showed the system boundary was already porous from the mainland side.
The 2019 extradition-bill protests put an estimated quarter to a third of the entire population into the streets, the gravest challenge to Beijing since 1989.
The 2020 National Security Law, imposed over Hong Kong's own legislature, ended the protests, the opposition, and the free press, and triggered a wave of Hong Kongers emigrating.
Taiwan, governed since 1949 by the side that lost the Chinese civil war, became a genuine democracy and elected the independence-leaning Tsai Ing-wen in 2016.
The 2014 Sunflower Movement turned Taiwanese politics against integration; Xi tied reunification to the rejuvenation timetable with record warplane incursions.
The central irony: crushing Hong Kong's two systems destroyed the very model that was supposed to win Taiwan peacefully.

title: The Wolf Warriors and the US Rivalry
bullets: The thirty-year assumption of engagement — that integration would make China more like its partners — failed, and both sides finally admitted it.
From 2018 the US imposed tariffs in a trade war citing the deficit, forced technology transfer, and the explicit threat of Made in China 2025.
The deeper conflict was a chip war: cutting China off from advanced semiconductors and the tools to make them, the single most serious external constraint on the model.
The arrest of Huawei's Meng Wanzhou and China's hostage-style detention of two Canadians made the technological war concrete; engagement gave way to 'decoupling'.
Wolf-warrior diplomacy traded soft-power patience for combative nationalism, hardening opinion across the democracies and accelerating the encirclement it reacted to.
China rejected a 2016 international tribunal ruling against its South China Sea claims and kept militarizing its artificial islands.
The 2020 Galwan Valley clash, the deadliest India–China violence in decades, was a brutal echo of the 1962 war China fought while its own people starved.
Galwan pushed India into the Quad, putting the two billion-person Asian giants — the democracy and the one-party state — on opposite sides of the century's defining rivalry.

title: COVID and the New Era
bullets: The book ends where the present begins: the stress test of the bargain — prosperity for a monopoly on power — too recent for verdicts.
The virus emerged in Wuhan in late 2019; officials silenced the doctor Li Wenliang, who was forced to recant, caught it, and died in February 2020.
The state pivoted to an unprecedented 76-day total Wuhan lockdown and a national zero-COVID apparatus of mass testing, snap lockdowns, and quarantine.
For two years zero-COVID genuinely worked at its stated goal and was broadcast as proof the system was superior to the death-tolerant democracies.
Then it inverted: the two-month lockdown of twenty-five million people in Shanghai broke the public's patience and the economy's momentum at once.
The November 2022 white-paper protests were the broadest open defiance since 1989, with a few voices calling for Xi himself to step down.
Zero-COVID was abandoned almost overnight in December 2022 with no preparation, producing an undercounted wave of one to two million deaths in months.

The 2022 congress gave Xi a third term; the property sector cracked with Evergrande, youth unemployment hit records, and the population began to shrink in 2022 — the prosperity half of the bargain now in doubt.
